[[画像:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|200px|多富気王子跡]] '''多富気王子'''（たふけおうじ）は[[九十九王子]]の最後の一社。[[大門坂]]登り口にほど近い、夫婦杉からすぐ上部のところにある。
起源や由緒など、不明なところが多い。那智山参詣の祓所とする説、「手向け」から転じたとする説や、この王子社を設けた那智山の社僧の名にちなむとする説（『那智勝浦町史』）などがある。史料上でその名が確認できるのは近世以降の地誌・旅行記の類においてのことであり、『紀南導郷記』では児宮の名が確認され、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』では若宮の名の他、道祖神を祭神とする旨の記述が見られる。
江戸時代には社殿があったと伝えられているが、[[1877年]]（[[明治10年]]）に熊野那智大社の[[摂末社|摂社]]のひとつ児宮として境内に移されたため、跡地には石碑と庚申塚のみが残されている。
==関連項目== *[[熊野信仰]] *[[九十九王子]] :[[市野々王子]] - '''多富気王子''' *[[那智山 (山)|那智山]]近辺の熊野信仰史跡 **[[熊野那智大社]] **[[青岸渡寺]] **[[大門坂]]
[[Category:九十九王子|たふけおうし]] [[Category:熊野信仰|熊野信仰]]

起源や由緒など、不明なところが多い。那智山参詣の祓所とする説、「手向け」から転じたとする説や、この王子社を設けた那智山の社僧の名にちなむとする説（『那智勝浦町史』）などがある。史料上でその名が確認できるのは近世以降の地誌・旅行記の類においてのことであり、『紀南導郷記』では児宮の名が確認され、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』では若宮の名の他、道祖神を祭神とする旨の記述が見られる。

[[画像:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|200px|多富気王子跡]] '''多富気王子'''（たふけおうじ）は、[[和歌山県]][[東牟婁郡]][[那智勝浦町]]にある[[神社]]旧蹟。[[九十九王子]]の最後の一社である。[[大門坂]]登り口にほど近い、夫婦杉からすぐ上部のところにある。
『[[中右記]]』、「熊野道之間愚記（『[[明月記]]』）、『[[藤原重子|修明門院]]参詣記』といった主要な中世参詣記には見られず、史料上で確認できるのは近世の地誌・旅行記の類においてである<ref name="kadokawa">「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編[1985: 665]</ref>。『紀南郷導記』（[[元禄]]年間）には近隣の市野々村の小字である二ノ瀬にある若一王子社の小祠として記述され、[[寛政]]6年（[[1794年]]）の『熊野巡覧記』には児宮とある。
起源や由緒など、不明なところが多い。「手向け」から転じたとする説や<ref name="heibon">平凡社[1997: 624]</ref>、この王子社を設けた那智山の社僧の名にちなむとする説（『那智勝浦町史』<ref name="kadokawa"/>）、那智山参詣の祓所とする説などがある。『[[紀伊続風土記]]』では若宮の名の他、道祖神を祭神とする旨の記述が見られる。江戸時代には社殿があったと伝えられているが、[[1877年]]（[[明治10年]]）に熊野那智大社の[[摂末社|摂社]]のひとつ児宮として境内に移され<ref name="heibon"/>、跡地には石碑と庚申塚のみが残されている<ref>西[1987: 172]</ref>。和歌山県指定史跡（昭和33年〈[[1958年]]〉4月1日指定）。
== 注 == {{Reflist}}

{{Template:九十九王子}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たふけおうし}} [[Category:九十九王子]] [[Category:那智勝浦町の神社]]

[[画像:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|200px|多富気王子跡]] '''多富気王子'''（たふけおうじ）は、[[和歌山県]][[東牟婁郡]][[那智勝浦町]]にある[[神社]]旧蹟。[[九十九王子]]の最後の一社である。[[大門坂]]登り口にほど近い、夫婦杉からすぐ上部のところにある。
『[[中右記]]』、「熊野道之間愚記（『[[明月記]]』）、『[[藤原重子|修明門院]]参詣記』といった主要な中世参詣記には見られず、史料上で確認できるのは近世の地誌・旅行記の類においてである<ref name="kadokawa">「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編[1985: 665]</ref>。『紀南郷導記』（[[元禄]]年間）には近隣の市野々村の小字である二ノ瀬にある若一王子社の小祠として記述され、[[寛政]]6年（[[1794年]]）の『熊野巡覧記』には児宮とある。
起源や由緒など、不明なところが多い。「手向け」から転じたとする説や<ref name="heibon">平凡社[1997: 624]</ref>、この王子社を設けた那智山の社僧の名にちなむとする説（『那智勝浦町史』<ref name="kadokawa"/>）、那智山参詣の祓所とする説などがある。『[[紀伊続風土記]]』では若宮の名の他、道祖神を祭神とする旨の記述が見られる。江戸時代には社殿があったと伝えられているが、[[1877年]]（[[明治10年]]）に熊野那智大社の[[摂末社|摂社]]のひとつ児宮として境内に移され<ref name="heibon"/>、跡地には石碑と庚申塚のみが残されている<ref>西[1987: 172]</ref>。和歌山県指定史跡（昭和33年〈[[1958年]]〉4月1日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web |author=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-03-20 }}</ref>。
== 注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5 * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ：熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）

[[画像:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|thumb|200px|多富気王子跡]] '''多富気王子'''（たふけおうじ、たぶけおうじ）は、[[和歌山県]][[東牟婁郡]][[那智勝浦町]]にある[[神社]]旧蹟。[[九十九王子]]の最後の一社である。[[大門坂]]登り口にほど近い、夫婦杉からすぐ上部のところにある。
『[[中右記]]』、「熊野道之間愚記」（『[[明月記]]』所収）、『[[藤原重子|修明門院]]参詣記』といった主要な中世参詣記には見られず、史料上で確認できるのは近世の地誌・旅行記の類においてである<ref name="kadokawa">「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編[1985: 665]</ref>。『紀南郷導記』（[[元禄]]年間）には近隣の市野々村の小字である二ノ瀬にある若一王子社の小祠として記述され、[[寛政]]6年（[[1794年]]）の『熊野巡覧記』には児宮とある。
起源や由緒など、不明なところが多い。「手向け」から転じたとする説や<ref name="heibon">平凡社[1997: 624]</ref>、この王子社を設けた那智山の社僧の名にちなむとする説（『那智勝浦町史』<ref name="kadokawa"/>）、那智山参詣の祓所とする説などがある。『[[紀伊続風土記]]』では若宮の名の他、道祖神を祭神とする旨の記述が見られる。江戸時代には社殿があったと伝えられているが、[[1877年]]（[[明治10年]]）に熊野那智大社の[[摂末社|摂社]]のひとつ児宮として境内に移され<ref name="heibon"/>、跡地には石碑と庚申塚のみが残されている<ref>西[1987: 172]</ref>。和歌山県指定史跡（昭和33年〈[[1958年]]〉4月1日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web |author=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-03-20 }}</ref>。
== 注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5 * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）

''{{PAGENAME}}'' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview==

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|qid=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|qid=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|qq=|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]] * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|qq=|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|qq=|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|qq=|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|qq=|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]] * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]] * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee|year=1985|title=Wakayama Prefecture|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|series=Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 30|isbn=404001300X}} * {{Cite book|author=Nishi Ritsu|year=1987|title=Kumano Kōdō Michishirube: Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji Genjō Tōsairoku|publisher=Arao Seibundō|series=Minamoto Series 1}} * {{Cite book|editor=Heibonsha|year=1997|title=Yamato-Kii Temple and Shrine Encyclopedia|publisher=Heibonsha|isbn=4582134025}}

== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]] * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]] * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]] * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}
