<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]] * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]] * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]] * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in [[:en:Nachikatsuura|Nachikatsuura]], [[:en:Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro District, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]], a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]], where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * [[:en:Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha]] * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura}}, {{ill|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|en|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|WD=Q1131175|lt=Higashimuro District, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}}, a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura}}, {{ill|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|en|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|WD=Q1131175|lt=Higashimuro District, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}}, a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430670]] Wikidata name: Tafuke-ōji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura}}, {{ill|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|en|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|WD=Q1131175|lt=Higashimuro District, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}}, a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura}}, {{ill|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|en|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|WD=Q1131175|lt=Higashimuro District, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}}, a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280}}}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura}}, {{ill|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|en|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|WD=Q1131175|lt=Higashimuro District, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}}, a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin}}, a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō}} (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|1=Nachikatsuura}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines}}}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, {{ill|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|en|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|WD=Q1131175|lt=Higashimuro District, Wakayama|1=Higashimuro District, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}}, a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin|1=Dōsojin}}, a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
== Related Sites == * {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}} (Nakahechi Route) * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} Shrines * {{ill|Ichinono Ōji|ja|市野々王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinono Ōji|1=Ichinono Ōji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11480731}} * {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:多富気王子]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Daimonzaka17 1024.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|1=Nachikatsuura}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.67138888888889|N|135.8985|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|part_of={{ill|Kujūku-ōji|WD=Q3200280|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines}}}} '''Tafuke Ōji''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]].
== Tafuke Ōji == '''Tafuke Ōji''' (多富気王子), also romanized as Tabu-ke Ōji, is a historical shrine site located in {{ill|Nachikatsuura|en|Nachikatsuura|WD=Q1346730|lt=Nachikatsuura|1=Nachikatsuura}}, {{ill|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|en|Higashimuro District, Wakayama|WD=Q1131175|lt=Higashimuro District, Wakayama|1=Higashimuro District, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. It is considered the final shrine in the sequence of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-nine Ōji Shrines) associated with the {{ill|Kumano Kodō|en|Kumano Kodō|WD=Q17050475|lt=Kumano Kodō|1=Kumano Kodō}}, a network of ancient pilgrimage routes. The site is located near the entrance of the {{ill|Daimonzaka|ja|大門坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daimonzaka|1=Daimonzaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3012117}}, just above the {{ill|Meoto Sugi|ja|夫婦杉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meoto Sugi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sacred Couple Cedars).
== Historical Background == Unlike many other Ōji shrines, Tafuke Ōji is not mentioned in prominent medieval pilgrimage records such as the ''{{ill|Chūyūki|ja|中右記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūyūki|qq=}}'', ''Kumano Dō no Aima Guki'' (熊野道之間愚記) included in the ''{{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}'', or the ''Shūmeimon-in Sankei-ki'' (修明門院参詣記). Its existence is confirmed primarily through Edo-period geographical texts and travel records.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref>
The ''Kinan Gōdōki'' (紀南郷導記) from the Genroku era describes it as a small shrine (shōshi) related to the nearby Wakaichi Ōji Shrine in the Ninose area of Ichinono Village. In the ''Kumano Junrō-ki'' (熊野巡覧記) from 1794, the site is referred to as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮, literally "Child Shrine").
== Origins and Significance == The origins of Tafuke Ōji remain unclear. There are several theories regarding its name and function: * It may derive from the term ''tamuke'' (手向け), meaning "offering" or "dedication."<ref name="heibon">Heibonsha (1997), p. 624.</ref> * Some suggest it was named after a local priest associated with Nachi Shrine.<ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Editorial Committee (1985), p. 665.</ref> * Another theory posits that it served as a purification site (haraisho) for pilgrims visiting Nachi Shrine.
The ''Kii Zoku Fudoki'' (紀伊続風土記) notes that it was dedicated to a {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōsojin|1=Dōsojin}}, a type of roadside deity protecting travelers. Although the shrine once had buildings during the Edo period, these were relocated in 1877 (Meiji 10) to the precincts of {{ill|Kumano Nachi Taisha|en|Kumano Nachi Taisha|WD=Q710359|lt=Kumano Nachi Taisha|1=Kumano Nachi Taisha}}, where it was known as ''Chigo-no-Miya'' (児宮). Only a stone monument and a {{ill|Kōshin-zuka|ja|庚申塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-zuka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庚申塔|1=Kōshin-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11486001}} (Kōshin Mound) now mark the original site.<ref>Nishi (1987), p. 172.</ref>
== Cultural Designation == Tafuke Ōji is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} by Wakayama Prefecture (designated on April 1, 1958).<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2023-10-17}}</ref>
